127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. 141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \

The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.

Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. 1	\chapter{FGA representability of the Picard scheme}
2	\label{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean
4	
5	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-174). This chapter is the A.2.c assembly sub-row of
6	% Route~A (positive-genus arm of nonempty_jacobianWitness). It wires the
7	% Quot-scheme engine (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}, A.2.b) and the
8	% \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	% A.1.c) into the FGA representability statement for \(\Pic_{C/k}\), following
10	% Kleiman, ``The Picard scheme'' \S 4 (\cite{kleiman-picard}). It is a small
11	% \emph{assembly} chapter: every nontrivial input is a \(\) pointer to a
12	% sibling chapter, and the work here is to thread the inputs through
13	% Grothendieck's existence theorem (\cite[\S 4, Main Theorem
14	% th:main]{kleiman-picard}) and its specialisation to a complete variety over a
15	% field (\cite[\S 4, Cor.~cor:algsch]{kleiman-picard}). Identity-component
16	% material (\Picz) is A.3 and lives in a separate sibling chapter --- it is
17	% \emph{not} scoped into this chapter.
18	
19	% NOTE (planner directive iter-174). This chapter must produce the
20	% \emph{representable Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) as a \(k\)-group scheme,
21	% locally of finite type, that represents the \'etale sheafification
22	% \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) defined in A.1.c. The three deliverables are
23	% (i) a line-bundle / Quot translation expressed via the Abel map
24	% \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (Kleiman \S 3, \S 4) which factorises through the
25	% Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{C/k} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_C/C/k}\), hence through the
26	% Quot engine of A.2.b; (ii) the existence theorem itself, in the
27	% field-of-definition specialisation \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper
28	% geometrically integral curve; (iii) the group-scheme refinement carried by
29	% the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The Lean
30	% encoding is delegated to a Lean file
31	% \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FGAPicRepresentability.lean} whose
32	% construction is described in the final section but not written in this
33	% chapter.
34	
35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:fga_pic_setup}
37	
38	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
39	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian). The relative
40	Picard presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Set\) of
41	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback} has already been packaged --- as a
42	set-valued functor at A.1.b, and refined to its abelian-group-valued
43	\'etale-sheafified version \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) at
44	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} (A.1.c). The Quot scheme machinery of
45	\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme} (A.2.b) supplies the representing engine: for
46	\(C/k\) projective and flat with integral geometric fibers, the Hilbert scheme
47	\(\Hilb_{C/k}\) and its open subscheme \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\) of relative effective
48	divisors are both representable by quasi-projective \(k\)-schemes. Grothendieck's
49	existence theorem for \(\Pic\) assembles these two ingredients via the Abel
50	map \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) to produce a representing scheme,
51	the \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The present chapter packages this
52	assembly: a line-bundle / Quot correspondence, the FGA representability
53	theorem, and the abelian-group-scheme refinement.
54	
55	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.4);
56	% arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26. Read from
57	% references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex.
58	
59	\section{Line bundles and the Quot / Hilbert scheme}
60	\label{sec:fga_pic_line_bundle_quot}
61	
62	The bridge from line bundles on \(C \times_k T\) to the Quot scheme is the
63	Abel map: a relative effective divisor \(D \subset C \times_k T\) over \(T\)
64	(an \(S\)-flat closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a closed
65	subscheme of \(C \times_k T\) flat over \(T\), hence a \(T\)-point of the Hilbert

158	\section{The FGA representability theorem}
159	\label{sec:fga_pic_representability}
160	
161	We now state and outline the proof of Grothendieck's existence theorem
162	for the Picard scheme, in the specialisation needed for the Jacobian arm
163	of Route~A: \(S = \Spec k\), \(X = C\) a smooth proper geometrically integral
164	curve. The general theorem (Kleiman \S 4 Thm.~th:main) covers any
165	\(X/S\) projective Zariski-locally over \(S\), flat, with integral geometric
166	fibers; we record the general statement and then specialise.
167	
168	\begin{theorem}\leanok
169	[FGA representability of the Picard scheme]
170	  \label{thm:fga_pic_representability}
171	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.representable}

223	  
224	  We follow Kleiman~\S 4 Thm.~th:main, specialised to \(S = \Spec k\) and
225	  \(X = C\). The proof proceeds in four conceptual steps; the underlying
226	  algebraic engine is the Quot scheme of \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
227	  and the \'etale-sheafification of \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
228	  together with the line-bundle / Quot correspondence of
229	  \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}.
230	
231	  \emph{Step 1: stratify by Hilbert polynomial.}
232	  For each polynomial \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\), let $P^\phi \subseteq
233	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\( be the \'etale subsheaf whose \)T$-points are
234	  represented by invertible sheaves \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) with
235	  \(\chi(C_t, \mathcal{L}_t^{-1}(n)) = \phi(n)\) for all \(t \in T\) (the
236	  Hilbert polynomial relative to the chosen polarisation
237	  \(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on the projective curve \(C\)). The \(P^\phi\) form a
238	  disjoint open cover of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (openness via Kleiman's
239	  flat-base-change argument; cf.\ EGA III 7.9.11). It suffices to
240	  represent each \(P^\phi\) by an increasing union of open quasi-projective
241	  \(k\)-schemes.
242	
243	  \emph{Step 2: bound twists by \(m\)-regularity.}
244	  Given \(\phi\), fix \(m\) large enough that the \(T\)-points of \(P^\phi\) are
245	  representable by \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C \times_k T\) satisfying
246	  \(R^i \pi_{T*} \mathcal{L}(n) = 0\) for all \(i \ge 1\), \(n \ge m\). Twisting
247	  by \(\mathcal{O}_C(m)\) defines an automorphism of \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
248	  that carries \(P^\phi\) to \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) for \(\phi_0(n) := \phi(m + n)\).
249	  Hence it suffices to represent each \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) by a quasi-projective
250	  \(k\)-scheme.
251	
252	  \emph{Step 3: factor through the Abel map.}
253	  Consider the Abel map \(A_{C/k} : \mathrm{Div}_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\)
254	  of \cref{lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence}. The source \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
255	  is representable by an open subscheme of \(\Hilb_{C/k}\) (and hence by an
256	  open subscheme of the Quot scheme of \cref{thm:quot_representable}), and
257	  is quasi-projective over \(k\). Pulling back along
258	  \(P^{\phi_0}_0 \hookrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) yields an open
259	  subscheme \(Z := P^{\phi_0}_0 \times_{\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}} \mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\)
260	  of \(\mathrm{Div}_{C/k}\), which is again quasi-projective. The projection
261	  \(\alpha : Z \to P^{\phi_0}_0\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves:
262	  given a \(T\)-point \(\lambda\) of \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) represented by an \'etale
263	  cover \(T' \to T\) and an invertible sheaf \(\mathcal{L}'\) on $C \times_k
264	  T'\( with \)H^1(C_{t'}, \mathcal{L}'_{t'}) = 0$, the projective bundle
265	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to T'\) associated to
266	  \(\mathcal{Q} := \pi_{T'*}\mathcal{L}'\) is smooth over \(T'\); an \'etale
267	  cover \(T_1 \to T'\) admits a \(T'\)-map \(T_1 \to \mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q})\)
268	  (EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii)) whose composition with the universal divisor map
269	  \(\mathbb{P}(\mathcal{Q}) \to Z\) produces the required \(T_1\)-point of \(Z\).
270	  Moreover, the first projection \(Z \times_{P^{\phi_0}_0} Z \to Z\) is
271	  smooth and proper.
272	
273	  \emph{Step 4: descend by the smooth-proper quotient lemma.}
274	  Apply Kleiman~\S 4 Lem.~lm:qt: given a surjection \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
275	  of \'etale sheaves with \(Z\) quasi-projective, \(R := Z \times_P Z\)
276	  representable, and the first projection \(R \to Z\) smooth and proper,
277	  \(P\) is representable by a quasi-projective scheme. In our case
278	  \(P = P^{\phi_0}_0\), \(Z\) and \(R\) as above, so \(P^{\phi_0}_0\) is
279	  quasi-projective. Reassembling over \(\phi \in \mathbb{Q}[n]\) and
280	  over \(m\), the disjoint union of representable \(P^\phi\) pieces represents
281	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\). The total scheme is separated (as a disjoint
282	  union of separated quasi-projectives) and locally of finite type. This
283	  is Kleiman~\S 4 Cor.~cor:algsch in the \(S = \Spec k\),
284	  \(X = C\) complete case.
285	\end{proof}

295	\begin{lemma}\leanok
296	[Smooth-proper quotient of \'etale sheaves]
297	  \label{lem:smooth_proper_quotient}

327	% "To lighten the notation, let \(Z\) and \(R\) denote the
328	% corresponding schemes as well.  Since the structure map \(Z\to S\) is

342	% [\dots]
343	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is flat, it is smooth if (and only if) its fibers are

351	% Since \(Z\to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves, \(Q\) is, in the
352	% category of \'etale sheaves, the coequalizer of the pair of maps

354	% elementary exercise in general Grothendieck topology.  By the same

356	% category, the coequalizer is unique up to unique isomorphism.  Thus \(Q\)

359	  By (ii) the structure map \(Z \to \Spec k\) is quasi-projective, hence
360	  separated, so the first projection \(Z \times_k Z \to Z\) is separated.
361	  By (iv) the first projection \(R \to Z\) is proper; it factors through
362	  \(h : R \to Z \times_k Z\) over the diagonal embedding (the second
363	  component is the second projection of \(R \to Z \times_P Z\)). The map
364	  \(h\) is therefore proper, and a monomorphism (because \(R\) is the graph
365	  of an equivalence relation on \(Z\), hence injective on \(T\)-points).
366	  By EGA IV 8.11.5, a proper monomorphism is a closed immersion: \(h\) is a
367	  closed embedding.
368	
369	  Hence \(R \hookrightarrow Z \times_k Z\) is a flat (by (iv)) and proper
370	  equivalence relation on the quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Z\). By the
371	  theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
372	  quasi-projective schemes (Altman--Kleiman; the construction places
373	  the graph \(R \subseteq Z \times_k Z\) inside the universal subscheme of
374	  the Hilbert scheme \(\Hilb_{Z/k}\) and descends to a closed subscheme
375	  \(Q \subseteq \Hilb_{Z/k}\) representing the quotient), there exist a
376	  quasi-projective \(k\)-scheme \(Q\) and a faithfully flat, projective map
377	  \(Z \to Q\) with \(R = Z \times_Q Z\).
378	
379	  The map \(Z \to Q\) is flat and proper; its fibers, up to field
380	  extension, coincide with those of \(R \to Z\), which are smooth by
381	  hypothesis. Hence \(Z \to Q\) is smooth.
382	
383	  Finally, \(Z \to Q\) is a surjection of \'etale sheaves (an \'etale
384	  cover of any \(T\)-point of \(Q\) pulls back to a \(T'\)-point of \(Z\), by
385	  EGA IV 17.16.3 (ii) applied to \(Z \times_Q T \to T\)). In the category
386	  of \'etale sheaves, both \(Q\) and \(P\) are coequalisers of the pair
387	  \(R \rightrightarrows Z\); the coequaliser is unique up to unique
388	  isomorphism, so \(Q\) represents \(P\). Smoothness of \(\alpha : Z \to P\)
389	  is the smoothness of \(Z \to Q\) just shown.
390	\end{proof}

392	\section{Group-scheme structure}
393	\label{sec:fga_pic_group_structure}
394	
395	The Picard scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) inherits an abelian group structure from
396	the tensor product of invertible sheaves on \(C \times_k T\). The relative
397	Picard presheaf \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\) is already an abelian
398	group at every \(T\) (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
399	functorially in \(T\); this lifts to the \'etale sheafification, and then
400	to a group-scheme structure on the representing scheme by Yoneda.

425	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
426	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."
427	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Def.~df:Pfs +
428	  \S 4, Def.~df:Psch (the Picard scheme).}
429	  Let \(\Pic_{C/k}\) be the Picard scheme of
430	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}. Then \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is canonically an
431	  abelian group scheme over \(k\): the tensor product
432	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}]\)
433	  and inverse \(-[\mathcal{L}] := [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]\) on
434	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) (well defined because both operations
435	  preserve isomorphism classes and descend through the subgroup
436	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined})
437	  are natural in \(T\), hence by Yoneda are represented by morphisms
438	  \(m : \Pic_{C/k} \times_k \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\),
439	  \(i : \Pic_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}\), and
440	  \(e : \Spec k \to \Pic_{C/k}\) (the trivial line bundle class) satisfying
441	  the abelian-group axioms. In particular, \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is a \(k\)-group
442	  scheme locally of finite type.
443	\end{theorem}
444	
445	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- Kleiman \S 2--4 do not isolate a
446	% separate proof block for the group-scheme structure: it is implicit in
447	% the fact that \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) takes values in abelian groups (df:Pfs)
448	% and that representability transfers algebraic operations from set-valued
449	% functors to scheme morphisms by Yoneda. The structural restatement below
450	% records the standard transfer.
451	\begin{proof}
452	  
453	  The relative Picard functor \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) of
454	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} is already abelian-group
455	  valued, with sum $[\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{M}] = [\mathcal{L} \otimes
456	  \mathcal{M}]\(, inverse \)-[\mathcal{L}] = [\mathcal{L}^{-1}]$, and unit
457	  \(0 = [\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}]\); these operations descend through
458	  the quotient by \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) because \(\pi_T^*\) is itself a
459	  group homomorphism. The \'etale sheafification step of
460	  \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} preserves the abelian-group structure
461	  on values, because sheafification of an abelian-presheaf is again an
462	  abelian-sheaf (the sheafification functor commutes with finite products
463	  in the value category).
464	
465	  By \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}, \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) is
466	  represented by the scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\). The Yoneda embedding is fully
467	  faithful and preserves limits, so the natural transformations
468	  $\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \times \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to
469	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ (multiplication),
470	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) (inverse), and the
471	  unit map \(h_{\Spec k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\) pick out unique
472	  morphisms of representing schemes \(m\), \(i\), \(e\) as displayed. The
473	  abelian-group axioms (associativity, commutativity, identity,
474	  inverse) hold at the level of \(T\)-points functorially in \(T\), hence
475	  hold on the representing scheme by Yoneda. The local-finite-type
476	  property is part of \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}.
477	\end{proof}
478	
479	\section{The Picard scheme: the named declaration}
480	\label{sec:fga_pic_named_definition}
481	
482	The two preceding theorems are existence statements. We record the
483	named declaration corresponding to the Lean target.
484	
485	\begin{definition}\leanok
486	[The Picard scheme]
487	  \label{def:pic_scheme}
488	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme}
489	  
490	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 4, Def.~df:Psch;
491	  % arXiv:math/0504020, p.~26 (read from
492	  % references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L2057-L2062).
493	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
494	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Psch}
495	  %    If any of the four relative Picard functors of Definition~REF
496	  %  is representable, then the representing scheme is called the {\it Picard
497	  %  scheme\/} and denoted by \(\IPic_{X/S}\).  Moreover, we say simply that the
498	  %  Picard scheme \(\IPic_{X/S}\) exists."

500	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a field
501	  \(k\). The \emph{Picard scheme} \(\Pic_{C/k}\) is the \(k\)-scheme representing

505	  locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of open

181	
182	The Picard scheme `Pic_{C/k}` of a smooth proper geometrically integral curve
183	`C/k` is the `k`-scheme representing the étale-sheafified relative Picard
184	functor `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}`. It is supplied by Kleiman §4 Thm. `th:main`
185	(specialised to `S = Spec k`, `X = C` via Cor. `cor:algsch`); the
186	representability theorem itself is the next declaration (`representable`),
187	and `PicScheme` is the witness scheme extracted from it.
188	
189	The scheme is separated, locally of finite type over `k`, and a disjoint
190	union of open quasi-projective `k`-subschemes; the abelian-group-scheme
191	structure is the content of `groupSchemeStructure`.
192	
193	Blueprint reference: `def:pic_scheme` (Kleiman §4 Def. `df:Psch`). -/
194	
195	/-- Typeclass asserting existence of a scheme over `Spec k` that represents
196	`picSharp C`. The single `⟨sorry⟩` instance below is the unique sorry-carrying
197	site for `PicScheme`; consumers that quantify over `[HasPicScheme C]` as a
198	hypothesis remain kernel-clean. -/

240	/-! ## §2. The Abel map — line-bundle / Quot correspondence
241	
242	The bridge from line bundles on `C ×_k T` to the Quot scheme is the **Abel
243	map**: a relative effective divisor `D ⊆ C ×_k T` over `T` (an `S`-flat
244	closed subscheme whose ideal is invertible) determines a `T`-point of the
245	Hilbert functor `Hilb_{C/k}(T)` (hence of `Div_{C/k}(T)`), and the dual
246	ideal sheaf `O_{C ×_k T}(D) := I_D⁻¹` is an invertible sheaf on the
247	relative curve, representing a class in `Pic^♯_{(C/k)ét}(T)`.
248	
249	The assignment `D ↦ [O_{C ×_k T}(D)]` is functorial in `T` (pullback of an
250	ideal sheaf along `id_C ×_k g : C ×_k T' ⟶ C ×_k T` takes the ideal of `D`
251	to the ideal of `D_{T'}`, since `D` is flat over `T` and hence
252	`D_{T'} = D ×_T T'` is again a relative effective divisor by Kleiman §3
253	Lem. `lm:ctn`).
254	
255	Blueprint reference: `lem:line_bundle_quot_correspondence` (Kleiman §3
256	Def. `dfn:Abel` + Thm. `th:repDiv`). -/

296	/-! ## §3. The smooth-proper quotient lemma — Altman–Kleiman descent
297	
298	The structural lemma underlying Step 4 of Kleiman §4 Thm. `th:main` is the
299	Altman–Kleiman **smooth-proper quotient lemma**: given a surjection
300	`α : Z ⟶ P` of étale sheaves with `R := Z ×_P Z` representable, `Z`
301	quasi-projective, and the first projection `R ⟶ Z` smooth and proper,
302	the quotient `P` is representable by a quasi-projective scheme.
303	
304	Kleiman's proof:
305	1. `R ↪ Z ×_k Z` is a flat (from (iv)) and proper monomorphism, hence a
306	   closed immersion by EGA IV 8.11.5.
307	2. By the theory of flat-and-proper effective equivalence relations on
308	   quasi-projective schemes (Altman–Kleiman), there exist a
309	   quasi-projective scheme `Q` and a faithfully flat, projective map
310	   `Z ⟶ Q` with `R = Z ×_Q Z`. The map `Z ⟶ Q` is smooth (its fibers
311	   coincide with those of `R ⟶ Z`, which are smooth).
312	3. In the category of étale sheaves, both `Q` and `P` are coequalisers of
313	   `R ⇒ Z`; uniqueness of the coequaliser gives `Q ≃ P`.
314	
315	Blueprint reference: `lem:smooth_proper_quotient` (Kleiman §4 Lem.
316	`lm:qt`). -/
317	
318	/-- **The smooth-proper quotient lemma — Altman–Kleiman descent of an
319	étale-sheaf surjection.**
320	
321	Let `Z, P : (Sch/k)^op ⥤ Type u` be presheaves on `(Sch/k)^op`, and let
322	`α : Z ⟶ P` be a natural transformation. Assume:
323	1. `Z` is representable by a `k`-scheme `Y` (encoded by the
324	   `Z.RepresentableBy Y` hypothesis),
325	2. the pullback presheaf `R := Z ×_P Z` is representable by a `k`-scheme `R`
326	   (encoded by the `(Limits.pullback α α).RepresentableBy R` hypothesis),
327	3. the first projection `π : R ⟶ Y` (after representation) is smooth and
328	   proper (encoded by the typeclass hypotheses `[Smooth π.left]` and
329	   `[IsProper π.left]`),
330	4. `α` is an étale surjection (encoded as the lift property: every
331	   `T`-point `p ∈ P(T)` lifts to a `T'`-point `z ∈ Z(T')` for some test
332	   morphism `T ⟶ T'`).
333	
334	Then `P` is representable (by a quasi-projective `k`-scheme `Q`).
335	
336	iter-177+: the body builds `Q` via the Altman–Kleiman effective quotient
337	of the flat-and-proper equivalence relation `R ↪ Y ×_k Y`, then exhibits
338	the coequaliser-uniqueness in the category of étale sheaves to identify
339	`Q` with the representing object of `P`. For the iter-176 file-skeleton
340	the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
341	theorem smoothProperQuotient {k : Type u} [Field k]
342	    {Z P : (Over (Spec (.of k)))ᵒᵖ ⥤ Type u}
343	    (α : Z ⟶ P)
344	    (Y : Over (Spec (.of k)))
345	    (_hZ : Z.RepresentableBy Y)
346	    (R : Over (Spec (.of k)))
347	    (_hR : (Limits.pullback α α).RepresentableBy R)
348	    (π : R ⟶ Y)
349	    [Smooth π.left] [IsProper π.left]
350	    (_hα : ∀ ⦃T : (Over (Spec (.of k)))ᵒᵖ⦄ (p : P.obj T),
351	        ∃ (T' : (Over (Spec (.of k)))ᵒᵖ) (e : T ⟶ T') (z : Z.obj T'),
352	          α.app T' z = P.map e p) :
353	    P.IsRepresentable := by
354	  sorry

411	/-! ## §5. The abelian group-scheme structure
412	
413	The Picard scheme `Pic_{C/k}` inherits an abelian group-scheme structure
414	from the tensor product of invertible sheaves on `C ×_k T`. The relative
415	Picard presheaf `T ↦ Pic(C ×_k T)/π_T^* Pic(T)` is already an abelian
416	group at every `T` (the quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup),
417	functorially in `T`; this lifts to the étale sheafification (sheafification
418	of an abelian-presheaf is an abelian-sheaf, since it commutes with finite
419	products in the value category), and then to a group-scheme structure on
420	the representing scheme by Yoneda.

1266	\section{The several Picard functors}\label{sc:Pfctrs}
1267	\renewcommand{\theequation}{\thethm.\arabic{equation}}
1268	Our first job is to identify a likely candidate for the functor of
1269	points of the Picard scheme.  In fact, there are several reasonable such
1270	Picard functors, and each one is more likely to be representable than
1271	the preceding.  In this section, they all are formally introduced and
1272	compared.
1273	
1274	\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
1275	 The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
1276	 category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
1277	abelian groups defined by the formula
1278		$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
1279	\end{dfn}
1280	
1281	The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
1282	sense: given any family of  $S$-schemes $T_i$, we have
1283		$$\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).$$
1284	 Hence, given a covering family $\{T_i\to T\}$ in the Zariski topology,
1285	the \'etale topology, or any other Grothendieck topology on the category
1286	of $S$-schemes, there is no harm, when we consider the sheaf associated
1287	to $\Pic_X$, in working simply with the single map $T'\to T$ where
1288	$T':=\coprod T_i$.  And doing so lightens the notation, making for
1289	easier reading. Therefore, we do so throughout, calling $T'\to T$ simply
1290	a {\it covering\/} in the given topology.
1291	
1292	The absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$ is never a separated presheaf in
1293	the Zariski topology.  Indeed, take an $S$-scheme $T$ that carries an
1294	invertible sheaf $\mc N$ such that $f^*_T\mc N$ is nontrivial.  (For
1295	example, take $T:=\I P^1_X$ and $\mc N:=\mc O_{\!T}(1)$.  Then the diagonal
1296	map $X\to X\x X$ induces a section $g$ of $f_T$; that is, $gf_T=1_T$.
1297	Hence $f^*_T\mc N$ is nontrivial.)  Now, there exists a Zariski covering
1298	$T'\to T$ such that the pullback $\mc N|T'$ is trivial; here, $T'$ is
1299	simply the disjoint union of the subsets in a suitable ordinary open
1300	covering of $T$.  So the pullback $f^*_{T'}\mc N|X_{T'}$ is trivial too.
1301	Thus $\Pic_X(T)$ has a nonzero element whose restriction is zero in
1302	$\Pic_X(T')$.
1303	
1304	According to descent theory, every representable functor is a sheaf in
1305	the Zariski topology \emdash in fact, in the \'etale and fppf
1306	topologies as well.  Therefore, the absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$ is never
1307	representable.  So, in the hope of obtaining a representable functor
1308	that differs as little as possible from $\Pic_X$, we now define a
1309	sequence of three successively more promising relative functors.
1310	
1311	\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
1312	  The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
1313		$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
1314	 Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
1315	topologies  by
1316		$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
1317		\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
1318	 \end{dfn}
1319	
1320	We now have a sequence of five Picard functors, and each one maps
1321	naturally into the next.  So each one maps naturally into any of its
1322	successors.  If the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
1323	then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
1324	associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is
1325	the natural map from a presheaf to its associated sheaf, as is easy to
1326	check.  In particular, the three displayed sheaves are the sheaves
1327	associated to the absolute Picard functor $\Pic_X$, as well as to the
1328	relative Picard functor $\Pic_{X/S}$.
1329	
1330	Since $\Pic_{X/S}$ is not a priori a sheaf, it is remarkable that it is
1331	representable so often in practice.
1332	
1333	Note that, for every $S$-scheme $T$, each $T$-point of
1334	$\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}$, or element of $\Pic_{(X/S)\fpqc}(T)$, is
1335	represented by an invertible sheaf $\mc L'$ on $X_{T'}$ for some
1336	fppf-covering $T'\to T$.    Moreover, there must
1337	be an fppf-covering $T''\to T'\x_T T'$ such that the two pullbacks of
1338	$\mc L'$ to $X_{T''}$ are isomorphic \emdash or to put it informally, the
1339	restrictions of $\mc L'$ must agree on a covering of the overlaps.
1340	
1341	Furthermore, a second such sheaf $\mc L_1$ on $X_{T_1}$ represents the
1342	same $T$-point if and only if there is an fppf-covering $T_1'\to T_1\x_T
1343	T'$ such that the pullbacks of $\mc L'$ and $\mc L_1$ to $X_{T_1'}$ are
1344	isomorphic.  Technically, this condition includes the preceding one,
1345	which concerns the case where $T_1=T'$ and $\mc L_1=\mc L'$ since $\mc
1346	L'$ must represent the same $T$-point as itself.  Of course, similar
1347	considerations apply to the Picard functors for the Zariski and \'etale
1348	topologies as well.

\paragraph{Mathlib prerequisites.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \texttt{Functor.RepresentableBy}, \texttt{Nonempty},

\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Route~C substrate dependency.} \textbf{Required}. The

required result is the curve specialisation of
Hartshorne~III.5.2 (Serre vanishing) combined with IV.1.3
(Riemann--Roch for curves). Without Riemann--Roch, the $m$-regularity
step cannot be discharged and the FGA construction does not
terminate.

re-engagement that the project STRATEGY.md flags as
substrate-blocked: once the Riemann--Roch arc lands, the $m$-regularity

remaining three steps (stratification, Abel-map factorisation,
smooth-proper quotient via Sorry~4) assemble into the existence
witness. Until then this sorry is the load-bearing blocker on the
Pic scheme arm of Route~A.

\paragraph{Closure-order rank.} \textbf{Rank 3} (requires the Route~C
substrate; load-bearing on the entire A.2.c representability claim).

\label{subsec:fga_pic_closure_order_summary}

The seven sorries partition into three ranks:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Rank 1} (independent, closeable from existing

        (\cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}) sibling chapters

        \texttt{divFunctor} pin.
      \item Sorry~3 (Abel map) is gated on Sorries~1 and~2.
      \item Sorry~4 (smooth-proper quotient) is the substantive
        structural lemma; closeable independently of the other six
        by formalising the EGA IV~8.11.5 step and the Altman--Kleiman
        descent.
    \end{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Rank 3} (requires the Route~C substrate; blocked
    until the Riemann--Roch arc lands): Sorries~5, 6, and~7.
    \begin{itemize}
      \item Sorry~5 (Picard scheme existence) is the load-bearing
        Route~C-blocked carrier via the $m$-regularity / Serre
        vanishing dependency.
      \item Sorry~6 (representability witness) inherits the Route~C
        dependency of Sorry~5.
      \item Sorry~7 (group-scheme structure) is Route~C-independent
        in itself but depends on Sorry~6.
    \end{itemize}
\end{itemize}

\medskip

\textbf{Recommended first attempt under the carrier-soundness
probe.} Sorry~4 (the \texttt{smoothProperQuotient} free \texttt{sorry})
is the highest-value rank-2 target: it is a structural lemma whose
discharge does not depend on any sibling chapter committing first,
and it removes the only non-instance sorry from the file. The EGA
IV~8.11.5 sub-step is bounded, and the Altman--Kleiman descent has a
known proof sketch in
\cref{lem:smooth_proper_quotient} of this chapter.

\textbf{Recommended second tier under the carrier-soundness probe.}
Sorries~1, 2, 3 (the carrier instances for
\texttt{PicSharp}, \texttt{divFunctor}, \texttt{abelMap}) are
discharged in lockstep once the sibling chapters
\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} and \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}
commit to their pins. The dispatch order across the three is
1~$\to$~2~$\to$~3 (the Abel map needs both presheaves to exist).

\textbf{Held until Route~C re-engagement.} Sorries~5, 6, 7 form a
single dependency chain (5~$\to$~6~$\to$~7) all gated on the
Riemann--Roch arc. The Route~C re-engagement is the strategic
prerequisite for any progress on these three carriers; until it
lands, the rank-2 work suffices to keep the carrier-soundness probe
structurally complete and to allow downstream Route~A consumers to
proceed under typeclass quantification.

## Notes for Plan Agent

### Directive line/identifier mismatch at L354

The directive labels L354 as "free `sorry` ... `Functor.IsRepresentable`
body for `representable` (the main A.2.c representability theorem)".
Reading the Lean file at the cited line shows that L354 is in fact
the `sorry` body of `smoothProperQuotient` (the theorem at L341
returning `P.IsRepresentable`), **not** of `representable`. The
declaration `representable` (L396) is a `noncomputable def` that
extracts via `Classical.choice` from
`PicSharpRepresentable.has_representable`; the sorry that backs it
lives in the typeclass instance `instPicSharpRepresentable` at
L408--409.

The blueprint section was written to match the **file's actual
structure** rather than the directive's attribution, because the
mathematical content of the L354 sorry (Altman--Kleiman descent of a
flat-and-proper equivalence relation) is structurally different from
the four-step FGA assembly that the directive ascribes to it. The
file's seven sorries are still:
- 1 free sorry: L354 (smoothProperQuotient body),
- 6 `⟨sorry⟩` instance bodies: L149, L176, L236, L294, L409, L465.
The directive's identifier label for L354 is the only mismatch.

### Carrier triviality at typeclass level

Four of the six `⟨sorry⟩` instances (Sorries 1, 2, 3, 7) have
typeclass body that unfolds to `Nonempty (some non-empty type)` —
trivially satisfied by any inhabitant of the type (e.g.\ a constant
functor, the zero natural transformation, etc.). This means the
typeclasses do **not** carry the semantic content their names
suggest: `HasPicSharp C` does not actually assert that the chosen
functor is the étale-sheafified relative Picard functor, only that
some presheaf of types exists. The new section flags this in each
subsection: closing the sorry to a tautological witness satisfies
the carrier-soundness probe (consumers under typeclass
quantification remain kernel-clean) but blocks downstream
mathematical use.

If the iter-198 review-phase abort verdict on the carrier-soundness
probe deems the typeclass abstraction unsound, the plan agent may
want to strengthen the typeclass bodies to require the specific
semantic witness (e.g.\ `HasPicSharp` should require a
`Functor.RepresentableBy`-style identification with a project-pinned
`PicSharp` rather than just `Nonempty`). This is a strategy-level
question and is recorded in "Strategy-modifying findings" below.

### Sibling-chapter pin lag

Sorries 1 and 2 cite the sibling chapters
`Picard_RelPicFunctor.tex` (A.1.c, `\lean{PicSharp}`) and
`Picard_QuotScheme.tex` (A.2.b, `\lean{divFunctor}`,
`\lean{QuotScheme}`) as the gating constraint. The new section
assumes those sibling chapters will land their Lean pins in a future
iteration but does not prescribe their structure beyond the names
already pinned by the `\lean{...}` hints. If the sibling-chapter
pins change name, the closure recipes for Sorries 1 and 2 will need
to be updated.

## Strategy-modifying findings

### Typeclass-level carrier triviality vs. semantic carrier soundness

The carrier-soundness refactor of iter-196 placed the six
representability carriers as `Prop`-valued typeclasses whose bodies
are `Nonempty (some type)` quantifications. For four of the six
(Sorries 1, 2, 3, 7) the typeclass body is **logically trivial**: it
can be discharged by a constant / zero witness that has nothing to
do with the named carrier (Pic^♯, Div, Abel map, group-object
structure). Only Sorries 5 and 6 (HasPicScheme and
PicSharpRepresentable) carry semantically non-trivial typeclass
bodies, because they quantify over a specific scheme `X` and over a
`RepresentableBy` witness that ties the functor and the scheme
together.

**Implication for STRATEGY.md.** The carrier-soundness probe's
soundness claim ("consumers quantifying over the typeclasses remain
kernel-clean") is technically correct, but its **strategic** value
is degraded: a downstream Route A consumer that quantifies over
`[HasPicSharp C]` and obtains a `picSharp C` via `Classical.choice`
gets a presheaf that is not actually committed to being the
étale-sheafified relative Picard functor. The downstream consumer
cannot prove anything mathematically interesting about this
`picSharp C` without further hypotheses tying it to the intended
semantic object.

The strategy may want to consider, in the iter-198 review-phase or
the next plan-phase strategy update, whether to:
1. Strengthen the four "trivial" typeclass bodies to require a
   semantic identification with the project-pinned sibling-chapter
   objects (when those pins land). This would re-couple the carriers
   but make downstream Route A consumers actually usable.
2. Accept the carrier-soundness probe as a purely structural device
   and document that no downstream mathematical claims can be made
   under `[HasPicSharp C]` quantification alone; consumers must
   additionally postulate the semantic identification as a separate
   typeclass.
3. Defer the question until the sibling chapters land, at which
   point the trivial typeclass bodies can be replaced with concrete
   re-export instances (which is what the existing Lean docstrings
   anticipate).

This is **not** a request to re-architect the probe (which the
directive explicitly forbids) but a flag that the carrier-soundness
abort verdict in iter-198 should distinguish between
"structurally sound carriers" (true for all six) and "semantically
meaningful carriers" (true for only Sorries 5 and 6 as currently
phrased). The blueprint section as written documents this
distinction for iter-199+ provers without altering the Lean code.
